get.description:
List tasks owned by the current API key. When `status` is omitted, the API returns active tasks only: `pending` and `processing`.

Use comma-separated filters, for example `operation=pdf_to_ppt&status=pending,processing&limit=20`. Results are ordered by `created_at` descending. When `has_more` is true, pass the oldest returned task's `created_at` value as `after` to fetch the next page.

parameters.name: operation
parameters.description: Comma-separated operations to include, for example pdf_to_ppt or image_to_design,pdf_to_design.

parameters.name: status
parameters.description: pending,processing

parameters.name: after
parameters.description: Pagination cursor. Pass the oldest returned created_at timestamp, or an RFC3339 timestamp.

get.description:
**Cost**: Free — task status query.

Get the latest state for one Open API task. The request must use the same API key that created the task. Terminal states are `succeeded`, `failed`, and `canceled`.

For `pdf_to_ppt`, the successful result contains `result.ppt_url` and may contain `result.note_slide_id`. For `image_to_design` and `pdf_to_design`, `result` contains the same design data shape returned by the existing synchronous endpoints.

parameters.name: task_id
parameters.description: Task ID returned by POST /v2/open/tasks.

post.description:
**Cost**: Free — cancellation refunds unused reserved credits.

Cancel a task that is still `pending` or `processing`. The request must use the same API key that created the task. Canceling marks the task `canceled` immediately, refunds unused reserved credits idempotently, and asks the worker to stop when it is running in the current process.

For `pdf_to_ppt`, credits are charged per converted page. If a 15-page task is canceled after 10 pages have already finished conversion, the cancel call refunds 65 credits and the task remains charged for 130 credits. If the task is still pending or has not converted any pages yet, all reserved credits are refunded. Non-page-based tasks refund their full reserved amount when canceled before completion.

The response includes `credits_reserved`, `credits_charged`, and `credits_refunded` so clients can show the final billing outcome.

A `succeeded` task returns HTTP 409 because completed work cannot be canceled. `failed` and `canceled` tasks return 200 with their current state.

parameters.name: task_id
parameters.description: Task ID returned by POST /v2/open/tasks.

409.description: Task already succeeded and cannot be canceled

post.description: Upload a local PDF for a follow-up Open API task. This endpoint returns an opaque `upload_id`, not a public file URL, so it cannot be used as free public S3 hosting. Pass the `upload_id` to `POST /v2/open/tasks` as `input.upload_id` for `pdf_to_ppt`. The upload is scoped to the same API key and expires after a short retention window.

file.description: PDF file to upload. The canonical field name is `file`.

pdf_file.description: Compatibility field name accepted for PDF uploads.

post.description: Estimate task credits before creating a task. For `pdf_to_ppt`, pass either `input.upload_id` from `/v2/open/uploads` or a caller-hosted `input.pdf_url`. Estimation does not create a task and does not deduct credits.

**⚠️ Deprecated — legacy v1 endpoint.** This path is retained only for existing integrations. New integrations should use `POST /v2/open/image_to_design` (v2 unified Open API). Input, output, and billing are unchanged — this endpoint keeps its own independent legacy credit SKU.

---

**Cost**: 13 credits per successful call.

Turn a single UI screenshot into an editable, hierarchical design tree — the same representation Codia Studio uses internally and the foundation that downstream code-generation, Figma-import, and layer-decomposition workflows are built on.

## Request options

This endpoint supports two input modes:

1. **JSON URL mode**: send `application/json` with `image_url` when the image is already publicly reachable.
2. **Direct upload mode**: send `multipart/form-data` with a local image file in the `image` field. The compatible field name `file` is also accepted. Codia checks credits before reading and uploading the file, so insufficient-credit requests return `402` without uploading anything.

JSON example:

```bash
curl 'https://api.codia.ai/v1/open/image_to_design' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer {codia_api_key}' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  --data '{ "image_url": "https://example.com/screenshot.png" }'
```

Direct upload example:

```bash
curl 'https://api.codia.ai/v1/open/image_to_design' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer {codia_api_key}' \
  -F 'image=@./screenshot.png'
```

## What this API is for

Given a public image URL of a UI (mobile screen, desktop page, dashboard, marketing hero — anything pixel-based), the service runs detection + layout reconstruction and returns a tree of **`VisualElement`** nodes that mirror how a designer would have built the screen in Figma or Sketch:

- Every visible region becomes a node with a position, a size, and a style.
- Container/child relationships are preserved (e.g. a card holds its title, body, and CTA).
- Layout intent (Flex vs. Absolute), text typography (font, size, weight, color), shapes, images, and effects are all reified into structured fields — not flattened into a single bitmap.

Typical use cases:
- **Image → code / image → Figma**: walk the tree and emit React / HTML / Figma nodes.
- **Design QA**: diff a screenshot against a spec by comparing structured trees.
- **Layer extraction**: feed the tree into downstream tools (e.g. our PDF/PPT pipeline or your own renderer).
- **Agent / LLM workflows**: give a model a structured layout instead of raw pixels so it can reason about "the price label sits inside the product card."

## How the response is shaped

The response uses the standard Codia envelope (`code`, `message`, `data`). On success `code = 0` and `data` contains two fields:

| Field | What it is | How to use it |
|---|---|---|
| `data.configuration` | Global rendering settings — `baseWidth`, `measurementUnit` (`px` \| `pt`), `scalingFactor`. | Multiply every numeric value in the tree by `scalingFactor` when rendering. Treat `baseWidth` as the canvas width the tree was measured against (375 for mobile, 1440 for desktop, etc.). |
| `data.visualElement` | The **root** of the visual tree. Always an `elementType: "Body"` node whose `childElements` recursively contain the rest of the page. | Traverse depth-first. The fields you care about per node depend on `elementType` (see below). |

## Walking the `VisualElement` tree

Each node has the same shape, but a handful of fields are populated based on `elementType`:

| `elementType` | Populated content fields | Typical meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `Body` | `layoutConfig`, `styleConfig` | Root of the page / artboard |
| `Layer` | `layoutConfig`, `styleConfig` | Generic container (card, section) |
| `Group` | `childElements` | Pure grouping, no own visuals |
| `Text` | `styleConfig.textConfig`, `contentData.textValue` | A run of text |
| `Image` | `contentData.imageSource` | Raster image (URL) |
| `Vector` | `contentData.vectorData` | Vector shape (mostly PDF; image_to_design uses `Image` / `Layer` for shapes) |
| `Component` | `componentSpec`, `contentData.componentReference` | Reusable detected component |

For every node:
- **`elementId` / `elementName`** — stable identifier + human label. Use `elementId` as your React `key` or Figma node id.
- **`childElements`** — array of nested `VisualElement`s. Empty leaf for text/image. Recurse here.
- **`processingMeta.detectionScore`** — confidence in `[0, 1]`. Filter or warn on low-confidence nodes when generating production code.
- **`processingMeta.surfaceArea`** — pixel area; useful for sorting or pruning tiny artifacts.

### `layoutConfig` — where the node sits

- `positionMode`:
  - `"Flex"` — the node participates in its parent's flex layout. Read `flexAttributes` (`flexDirection`, `alignItems`, `justifyContent`, `flexWrap`) on the **parent** to know how children flow.
  - `"Absolute"` — positioned at exact coordinates. Read `absoluteAttrs.coord` for the offset relative to the parent.
  - `"Normal"` / `"Relative"` — document-flow positioning (rare on this endpoint; common at the root).
- `flexibleMode` — present on containers, identifies the flex variant the detector chose.

In practice, the root `Body` is `Flex` and most children inherit Flex from their parent, so a naive emitter that maps `positionMode` → CSS `display: flex` + `position: absolute` will reproduce the layout faithfully.

### `styleConfig` — what the node looks like

This is the largest object in the response. Group the fields by what they describe:

- **Size** — `widthSpec`, `heightSpec`. Each is a `DimensionSpec` with `sizing` (`FIXED` = exact px, `FILL` = stretch to parent, `FIT_CONTENT` = shrink to children) and a numeric `value`. Map `FIXED → width: Npx`, `FILL → flex: 1` (or `width: 100%`), `FIT_CONTENT → width: auto`.
- **Text** (on `Text` nodes) — `textConfig.{fontSize, fontStyle, fontFamily, lineHeight, letterSpacing, textAlign}` + `textColor` (a `VisualColor` with `rgbValues` and `hexCode`). `letterSpacing` is in CSS px against the detected font-size; Studio converts to a percentage at import time (Figma semantics).
- **Border** — `borderConfig.{borderWidth, borderStyle, borderColor, borderRadius}`. `borderRadius` is an array `[tl, tr, br, bl]`; collapse to a single value if all four are equal.
- **Background** — `backgroundConfig` is a `oneOf` with three shapes discriminated by `type`:
  - `"COLOR"` → solid fill via `backgroundColor`.
  - `"IMAGE"` → `imageUrl` + CSS-style `backgroundSize` / `backgroundPosition`.
  - `"LINEAR_GRADIENT"` → `deg` + `gradientStops[]` (each stop = `{color, position}` where `position` is 0–1).
- **Effects / transforms** — `effectsList` (shadows, blurs), `opacityLevel` (0–255 — divide by 255 for CSS), `rotationAngle` (degrees), `paddingValues`, `overflowMode`.

Fields tagged "PDF only" in the schema (`characterData`, `transformMatrix`, `cornerRadius`, `isRightToLeft`, `extendedMatrix`) are not produced by image_to_design — they're shared with `pdf_to_design` and you can ignore them here. Same for `boundingBox`, `displayOrder`, and `contentData.{svgTemplate, imageData, maskingMode, blendingType}`.

### `contentData` — what the node displays

Only populated on leaf-ish nodes:
- `textValue` — the actual string for `Text` nodes.
- `imageSource` — public URL for `Image` nodes (re-hosted on Codia's CDN; safe to embed).
- `componentReference` / `componentAttributes` — pointer + props for detected components, paired with `componentSpec` on the same node.

## A minimal traversal

```ts
function walk(node: VisualElement, depth = 0) {
  console.log('  '.repeat(depth), node.elementType, node.elementId,
    node.elementType === 'Text' ? node.contentData?.textValue :
    node.elementType === 'Image' ? node.contentData?.imageSource : '');
  node.childElements?.forEach(child => walk(child, depth + 1));
}
walk(response.data.visualElement);
```

## Inputs, limits, billing

- **Input** — either JSON with a single `image_url` pointing to a publicly fetchable PNG / JPG / WebP, or `multipart/form-data` with an `image` file. For direct upload, Codia checks credits before reading and uploading the file. Field name `file` is also accepted for compatibility. Maximum side length and file-size limits follow the global Codia API limits.
- **Billing** — consumes the unified Codia Open API balance (see `GET /v2/open/credits`). 13 credits per successful call.
- **Latency** — synchronous; typical end-to-end is single-digit seconds for mobile screens, longer for dense desktop pages.
- **Idempotency** — same image URL produces the same tree modulo `elementId` regeneration; do not rely on stable IDs across calls.

## Errors

Uses the standard envelope with non-zero `code` and a descriptive `message`. HTTP status codes:
- `400` — bad input (missing/invalid `image_url`, unreachable URL, unsupported MIME).
- `401` / `403` — missing / wrong / unauthorized API key.
- `402` — credit balance exhausted (top up via the dashboard or `/v2/open/auto_recharge`).
- `429` — rate-limited; back off and retry.
- `500` — internal error; safe to retry once.

### `measurementUnit`

Always `px` for `image_to_design` — screen captures and rasterised UI are inherently pixel-based.

> **Sync vs. async**
> This synchronous endpoint returns the result inline — the simplest path. For production pipelines that need webhook delivery, automatic retry with idempotency, or explicit task cancellation, call `POST /v2/open/tasks` with `operation=image_to_design` instead. Inputs and credit cost are identical to this endpoint.

image_url.description: Public URL of the image to analyze. Must be a directly fetchable PNG, JPG, or WebP — pre-signed S3 or CDN URLs are fine, but pages that gate the asset behind login or JavaScript will fail. There is no separate `image_base64` field; host the image first.

image.description: Local image file to analyze. Use this when the image is not already hosted. The server checks credits before reading and uploading the file. Field name `file` is also accepted for compatibility.

200.description: Successful conversion. `data.visualElement` is the root `Body` node of the tree — recurse through `childElements` to reach every detected region. `data.configuration` carries the canvas base width, unit, and a scale factor you should multiply every numeric value by before rendering. See the operation description above for a full walkthrough of `elementType`, `layoutConfig`, `styleConfig`, and `contentData`.

post.description:
**⚠️ Deprecated — legacy v1 endpoint.** This path is retained only for existing integrations. New integrations should use `POST /v2/open/pdf_to_design` (v2 unified Open API). Input, output, and billing are unchanged — this endpoint keeps its own independent legacy credit SKU.

---

**Cost**: 13 credits per processed page.

Extract a structured, editable design tree out of one or more pages of a PDF. Each page becomes a fully-populated **`VisualElement`** tree — the same node shape as `image_to_design` — augmented with the vector / typography / transform fields that only PDFs carry.

## What this API is for

PDFs are vector documents: every paragraph, every shape, every gradient is stored as math, not pixels. This endpoint preserves that math instead of rasterizing it, so the tree you get back can be re-rendered, re-flowed, re-styled, or translated into another format without quality loss.

Typical use cases:
- **PDF → editable design**: open up an exported brochure / spec sheet / one-pager and turn it back into a Figma-like tree.
- **PDF → PPT / HTML / code**: walk the tree to emit slides, web pages, or component code. (Our own `pdf_to_ppt` builds on this.)
- **Content extraction**: pull every text run with its true font, size, and color, plus every vector shape with its actual path data.
- **Asset salvage**: re-export embedded images and vector logos at original resolution.

Differences vs. `image_to_design`: input is a **PDF URL or PDF file**, output is **one tree per requested page** (not a single tree), and the per-node fields tagged "PDF only" in the shared schema **are** populated here (vector paths, character-level styling, transform matrices, etc.).

## Sending the request

This endpoint supports two input modes:

1. **JSON URL mode**: send `application/json` with `pdf_url` and `page_no` when the PDF is already publicly reachable.
2. **Direct upload mode**: send `multipart/form-data` with a local PDF file in the `pdf_file` field and one or more `page_no` fields.

JSON example:

```bash
curl 'https://api.codia.ai/v1/open/pdf_to_design' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer {codia_api_key}' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  --data '{ "pdf_url": "https://example.com/file.pdf", "page_no": [0, 2, 5] }'
```

Direct upload example:

```bash
curl 'https://api.codia.ai/v1/open/pdf_to_design' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer {codia_api_key}' \
  -F 'pdf_file=@./file.pdf' \
  -F 'page_no=0' \
  -F 'page_no=2'
```

- **`pdf_url`** *(JSON mode, required)* — public URL of the PDF. Encrypted / password-protected PDFs are rejected.
- **`pdf_file`** *(upload mode, required, binary)* — the PDF itself. Stream the file directly; do not base64-encode it.
- **`page_no`** *(required)* — **0-indexed** page numbers. JSON mode uses an integer array; multipart mode repeats the `page_no` field once per page. Page count and file-size caps follow the global Codia API limits.

## Response shape

Standard Codia envelope; on success `data` carries:

| Field | What it is | How to use it |
|---|---|---|
| `data.configuration` | Same `Configuration` object as `image_to_design` — `baseWidth`, `measurementUnit` (`px` \| `pt`), `scalingFactor`. | Apply `scalingFactor` to every numeric value when rendering. `baseWidth` is the canvas width the trees were measured against (often page width in points or px, e.g. 1940). |
| `data.size` | Page dimensions in document units: `{ width, height }`. | Use to set up artboard / viewport per page. All pages in a single PDF share this size. |
| `data.pages[]` | One entry per requested page. Each entry is `{ visualElement: <Body root> }`. | Iterate pages in order; recurse into each `visualElement.childElements` to reach every region. |

## Walking a page tree

Each `data.pages[i].visualElement` is a `Body`-typed root with the same `VisualElement` shape used elsewhere in this API (see `image_to_design` for the full field guide). The headline pieces:

- **`elementType`** — `Body` at the root, then `Layer` / `Group` / `Text` / `Image` / `Vector` / `Component` inside. PDFs use `Vector` heavily for shapes, gradients, and logos.
- **`childElements`** — nested nodes; recurse depth-first.
- **`layoutConfig` / `styleConfig` / `processingMeta`** — same semantics as `image_to_design`.
- **`contentData`** — for `Text` reads `textValue`; for `Image` reads `imageSource` and/or the base64 in `imageData`; for `Vector` reads `vectorData` (see below).

### PDF-specific fields you can actually use

Fields the shared schema tags "PDF only" — these are real, populated, and useful on this endpoint:

- **`boundingBox`** *(`[x, y, width, height]`)* — exact node geometry in document coordinates. The most reliable positioning source for PDFs.
- **`displayOrder`** — z-order within the page; lower = drawn first.
- **`styleConfig.transformMatrix` / `extendedMatrix`** — affine transform applied to the node. `transformMatrix` is the 6-element PDF form `[a b c d e f]`; `extendedMatrix` is the CSS-compatible string. Apply this to handle rotated text, sheared images, etc.
- **`styleConfig.characterData`** — per-character styling keyed by character index. Use this when a single text node mixes fonts, sizes, or colors (very common in PDFs).
- **`styleConfig.cornerRadius`** — scalar fallback when the four-corner array isn't applicable.
- **`styleConfig.isRightToLeft`** — direction flag for Arabic / Hebrew text.
- **`contentData.vectorData`** — the actual vector path. Contains `pathItems[]` (each item is `{ pathType: "l" | "c", coordinates[] }` — `l` = line, `c` = cubic curve), plus `fillColor`, `strokeColor`, `strokeWidth`, `fillOpacity`, `strokeOpacity`, `lineCapStyle`, `lineJoinStyle`, `dashPattern`, `evenOddRule`, `isClosedPath`. Emit SVG `<path>` directly, or rebuild in your own renderer.
- **`contentData.imageData`** — base64-encoded image bytes for embedded raster images (alongside or instead of `imageSource`).
- **`contentData.svgTemplate`** — pre-rendered SVG markup template for compound vector content.
- **`contentData.maskingMode` / `blendingType`** — PDF graphics-state knobs (clip / soft-mask, blend mode). Map to CSS `mix-blend-mode` / SVG `<mask>` as appropriate.

### A minimal traversal

```ts
for (const page of response.data.pages) {
  console.log('page', page.visualElement.elementName, response.data.size);
  walk(page.visualElement);
}

function walk(node, depth = 0) {
  const pad = '  '.repeat(depth);
  if (node.elementType === 'Text') console.log(pad, 'TXT', node.contentData?.textValue);
  if (node.elementType === 'Image') console.log(pad, 'IMG', node.contentData?.imageSource);
  if (node.elementType === 'Vector') console.log(pad, 'VEC', node.contentData?.vectorData?.pathItems?.length, 'segs');
  node.childElements?.forEach(c => walk(c, depth + 1));
}
```

## Inputs, limits, billing

- **Input** — either JSON with `pdf_url` or `multipart/form-data` with `pdf_file`. Encrypted / password-protected PDFs are rejected.
- **Page selection** — `page_no` uses **0-based** indices. JSON mode sends an integer array; multipart mode repeats the `page_no` field once per page. Out-of-range indices are silently dropped from the response.
- **Billing** — consumes the unified Codia Open API balance (`GET /v2/open/credits`). 13 credits per processed page.
- **Latency** — synchronous. Throughput is per-page; a long document may take many seconds. For very long PDFs, batch the request by `page_no` ranges and parallelize.
- **Determinism** — same PDF + same `page_no` yields the same structural tree, but `elementId`s are regenerated each call; don't rely on cross-call ID stability.

## Errors

Uses the standard envelope with non-zero `code` and a descriptive `message`. HTTP status codes:
- `400` — bad input (missing `pdf_url` / `pdf_file`, malformed `page_no`, corrupt / encrypted PDF).
- `401` / `403` — missing / wrong / unauthorized API key.
- `402` — credits exhausted (see `/v2/open/auto_recharge`).
- `429` — rate-limited; back off and retry.
- `500` — internal error; safe to retry once.

### `measurementUnit`

`pdf_to_design` returns whichever unit the source PDF used. Most editorial / business PDFs report `pt` (PostScript points: 72 = 1 inch). PDFs exported from design tools at a specific pixel target may report `px`. Read this field per response and convert if your downstream renderer assumes one unit.

> **Sync vs. async**
> This synchronous endpoint returns the result inline — fine for short PDFs. For documents with more than ~5 pages, prefer `POST /v2/open/tasks` with `operation=pdf_to_design` to avoid HTTP client timeouts, get webhook delivery, and support cancellation. Inputs and credit cost are identical to this endpoint.

pdf_url.description: Public URL of the PDF to process. Must be directly fetchable by Codia.

pdf_file.description: The PDF file to process. Sent as a multipart binary part.

page_no.description: Repeat the multipart page_no field once per 0-indexed page to process.

200.description: Successful conversion. `data.pages` is an array — one entry per processed page, each containing a `visualElement` tree with the same `VisualElement` shape as `image_to_design` (plus the PDF-only fields documented in the operation description). `data.size` carries the page dimensions and `data.configuration` carries the canvas scale / unit.

post.description:
**⚠️ Deprecated — legacy v1 endpoint.** This path is retained only for existing integrations. New integrations should use `POST /v2/open/remove_bg` (v2 unified Open API). Input, output, and billing are unchanged — this endpoint keeps its own independent legacy credit SKU.

---

**Cost**: 13 credits per successful call.

Remove Background supports two input modes:

1. **JSON URL mode**: send `application/json` with the existing URL field.
2. **Direct upload mode**: send `multipart/form-data` with a local image file in the `image` field. The compatible field name `file` is also accepted for the primary image. Codia checks credits before reading and uploading the file, so insufficient-credit requests return `402` without uploading anything.

Direct upload example:

```bash
curl 'https://api.codia.ai/v1/open/remove_bg' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer {codia_api_key}' \
  -F 'image=@./product.png'
```

Cut out the foreground subject of an image and return a PNG with a fully transparent background. A synchronous, single-purpose endpoint — no model parameter, no prompt, no options.

## What this API is for

Product photos for storefronts, profile avatars, sticker generation, compositing pipelines — any time you need the subject without its scene. Returns a true alpha-channel PNG (not a flat white background).

## Request

| Field | Required | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `image_url` | yes | Public URL of the image to process. PNG, JPG, or WebP. Must be reachable from Codia's network. |

## Response

Standard envelope. Unique to this endpoint (not the shared `ImageUrlListSuccess`): `data.image_url` is a **single** string — a CDN URL to the processed transparent PNG. Download promptly; the URL is pre-signed.

```json
{ "code": 0, "message": "ok", "data": { "image_url": "https://cdn.codia.ai/bg-removed/abc.png" } }
```

## Billing & limits

- 13 credits per successful call, from the unified Codia Open API balance.
- Synchronous; typically completes within a few seconds.
- Output is always PNG with an alpha channel, at the input image's resolution.

## Errors

Standard set — `400` for bad input, `401`/`403` auth, `402` credits, `429` rate limit, `500` internal.

> **Sync vs. async**
> This synchronous endpoint returns the result inline — the simplest path. For production pipelines that need webhook delivery, automatic retry with idempotency, or explicit task cancellation, call `POST /v2/open/tasks` with `operation=remove_bg` instead. Inputs and credit cost are identical to this endpoint.
